description: The untouchable era. From Homer's Odyssey to You Only Move Twice. When nothing could go wrong.

episodes.title: Simpsons Roasting on an Open Fire
episodes.description: Homer's Christmas bonus is canceled and the rainy-day funds are needed to pay for Bart's tattoo removal.
episodes.notes: This Christmas special served as The Simpsons' official series premiere, making it the longest-running American primetime scripted series that began with a holiday episode.

episodes.title: Bart the Genius
episodes.description: Bart's labeled a genius and sent to a school for the gifted.

guests.character: Milhouse Van Houten / Ethan Foley / Boy (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets squeezed together until they pop up into the air.
episodes.notes: This was the first full-length Simpsons episode to air, establishing the show's format after the Christmas special "Simpsons Roasting on an Open Fire.

episodes.title: Homer's Odyssey
episodes.description: After being fired from the nuclear power plant for negligence, Homer becomes a safety activist.

guests.character: Sherri / Terri / Inanimate Carbon Rod #1 / Mrs. Winfield (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and it tips over backwards.
episodes.notes: This episode marks the first time Homer's job at the nuclear plant is central to the plot, establishing the workplace dynamic that would become crucial to the series.

episodes.title: There's No Disgrace Like Home
episodes.description: After seeing families being nice to each other at a company picnic, Homer orders the Simpsons to a family therapist.

guests.character: Mother #1 / Daughter / Mother in Monroe ad (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets shocked by an electric buzzer.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced Dr. Marvin Monroe, the family therapist who would become a recurring character until his retirement from the show in Season 4. The family therapy session featuring shock therapy became one of the early series' most memorable gags.

episodes.title: Bart the General
episodes.description: Terrorized by the school bully, Bart seeks counsel from Grandpa Simpson, who takes him to a warfare expert for basic training.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets squeezed together until they pop up into the air.
episodes.parody_of: War films and military training movies
episodes.notes: This episode introduced Nelson Muntz and his iconic "Ha-ha!" laugh, which became one of the most recognizable catchphrases in television history.

episodes.title: Moaning Lisa
episodes.description: The meaning of life and second grade have Lisa playing the blues solo, until she jams with a kindred spirit; Homer seeks tutelage to beat Bart at video boxing.

guests.character: Janey Powell / Boy with Howie / Howie's Mother (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions.
episodes.notes: Ron Taylor's soulful performance as Bleeding Gums Murphy introduced Lisa's lifelong love of jazz and became one of the series' most beloved mentor-student relationships. This marked the first episode to deeply explore Lisa's character beyond her role as the smart middle child.

episodes.title: The Call of the Simpsons
episodes.description: The Simpsons put the wild back in the wilderness when their RV goes over a cliff and Homer and Bart go for help, get lost and lose their clothes.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions.
episodes.parody_of: Bigfoot documentaries and wilderness survival stories
episodes.notes: This episode marks the first time Homer is mistaken for Bigfoot, establishing a recurring gag that would appear throughout the series whenever Homer is seen naked or disheveled in nature.

episodes.title: The Telltale Head
episodes.description: Bart pulls a stunt that shocks the entire town, including the new friends he was trying to impress.

guests.character: Milhouse Van Houten / Janey Powell / Dolph (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets squeezed together like a accordion.
episodes.notes: This episode marks the first time Bart's pranks have serious consequences that affect the entire town, establishing a template for future episodes where his actions spiral beyond his control.

episodes.title: Life on the Fast Lane
episodes.description: Homer's bowling-ball gift "for Marge" prompts her to take up the sport, and maybe also a handsome instructor who offers to teach her "many things."
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets squeezed together until they pop up into the air.
episodes.parody_of: The Bridge of Madison County and romantic drama films
episodes.notes: This marks the first time The Simpsons explored marital infidelity as a serious dramatic theme, establishing the template for the show's ability to balance comedy with genuine emotional stakes.

episodes.title: Homer's Night Out
episodes.description: Shutterbug Bart captures Homer with a sexy entertainer at a bachelor party.

guests.character: Martin Prince / Photography Club Member #3 / Lewis (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets crushed by a foot from above.
episodes.notes: First appearance of Princess Kashmir, the belly dancer who becomes central to Homer's marital crisis, marking one of the earliest episodes to explore Homer and Marge's relationship dynamics in depth.

episodes.title: The Crepes of Wrath
episodes.description: A little story about how Bart saves France after blowing up Principal Skinner's mom with a cherry bomb.

guests.character: Agnes Skinner / Adil Hoxha / Airport Announcer (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.notes: This episode marks the first time Bart travels outside of Springfield, establishing the template for future international adventures that would become a recurring series theme.

episodes.title: Krusty Gets Busted
episodes.description: Bart's TV hero Krusty the Clown is arrested for a convenience-store robbery, and witness Homer must testify against the clown.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets squeezed together like a accordion.
episodes.notes: First appearance of Sideshow Bob, who would go on to appear in 14 more episodes and become one of the series' most iconic recurring villains.

episodes.title: Some Enchanted Evening
episodes.description: Homer takes Marge out for a romantic night, leaving the kids with a sitter, who's profiled on "America's Most Armed and Dangerous."

guests.character: Rubber Baby Buggy Bumper Babysitting / Happy Little Elf (voice)
America's Most Armed and Dangerous Presenter (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and it tips over backwards.
episodes.notes: This was the intended series finale before The Simpsons was renewed, making it the first episode specifically written to potentially end the show.

episodes.title: Bart Gets an 'F'
episodes.description: Bart fails four consecutive history exams and the school psychiatrist recommends that Bart repeat the fourth grade.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets flipped upside down.
episodes.notes: This Season 2 premiere became the highest-rated episode in Simpsons history at the time, drawing over 33 million viewers and proving the show's staying power after its successful first season.

episodes.title: Simpson and Delilah
episodes.description: Homer uses his medical insurance to buy Dimoxinil, a miracle hair-growth formula. After growing luxurious locks, he's given a promotion which allows him to hire a secretary named Karl and makes grand promises to his family at home.

guests.character: Milhouse Van Houten / Applicant / Woman in Audience (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions, then bounced back out.
episodes.notes: Harvey Fierstein voiced Karl, Homer's devoted secretary, in one of the show's most memorable single-episode character performances. The episode established the recurring gag of Homer's baldness being a source of insecurity, which would become a running theme throughout the series.

episodes.title: Treehouse of Horror
episodes.description: The Simpson kids hide in the family treehouse and tell each other spooky stories. The first regards haunted houses, the second introduces Kang and Kodos for the first time in an alien abduction, and the third is a Simpson-adapted retelling of The Raven.

guests.character: Moving Man / Serak the Preparer / Narrator (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and sinks into the floor.
episodes.parody_of: Edgar Allan Poe's The Raven and classic horror anthology shows
episodes.notes: This episode launched The Simpsons' beloved annual Halloween tradition and introduced iconic alien characters Kang and Kodos, who would become recurring fixtures in every subsequent Treehouse of Horror episode.

episodes.title: Two Cars in Every Garage and Three Eyes on Every Fish
episodes.description: Bart catches a three-eyed fish in a river downstream of the Nuclear Power Plant. This causes the plant to become inspected, and in order to prevent it from being shut down, Mr. Burns decides to run for governor. After a hard campaign which sees Burns rise from being universally despised to running neck and neck with popular incumbent Mary Bailey, it is decided that Burns will have dinner with a random employee the night before the election. Homer is chosen, much to Marge's chagrin.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets flipped upside down along with the couch.
episodes.parody_of: Political campaign films and election coverage
episodes.notes: This episode introduced Blinky, the three-eyed fish who became a recurring symbol of Springfield's environmental problems and one of the show's most recognizable minor characters.

episodes.title: Dancin' Homer
episodes.description: Homer fires up the crowd at a Springfield Isotopes baseball game and is chosen to be the team's new mascot. He immediately becomes a popular attraction and the Isotopes start a winning streak. As a result, Homer is promoted to a team in Capital City, the Capital City Capitals.

guests.character: Milhouse Van Houten / Janey Powell / Ex-Wife (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and bounces up and down like they're on a trampoline.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced the beloved song "Capital City," which became one of the series' most memorable musical numbers and established the show's tradition of elaborate musical sequences.

episodes.title: Dead Putting Society
episodes.description: Bart and Todd Flanders decide to enter a miniature golf tournament. Homer becomes confident that Bart will win and makes a bet with Ned that the father of the boy that does not win will have to mow their neighbors lawn in their wife's Sunday dress.

guests.character: Maude Flanders / Helen Lovejoy / Mrs. Norton (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and it tips backward, flipping them upside down.
episodes.notes: This episode marks the first time Homer and Ned Flanders engage in direct competition, establishing their rivalry dynamic that would become a cornerstone of the series.

episodes.title: Bart vs. Thanksgiving
episodes.description: Lisa makes a table centerpiece for the Thanksgiving dinner, which Bart accidentally destroys. After he is sent to his room by his parents, Bart angrily runs away from home and experiences a very different kind of Thanksgiving holiday.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets squeezed together like an accordion.
episodes.notes: This was The Simpsons' first holiday-themed episode, establishing the template for the show's annual tradition of Christmas and Thanksgiving specials.

episodes.title: Bart the Daredevil
episodes.description: The Simpsons go to a Monster truck rally that features famous daredevil Lance Murdock. Bart immediately becomes enamored and decides that he wants to become a daredevil as well. His first stunt ends in injury, but to gain the respect of his friends, he promises a far more impressive feat to them: to jump the Springfield Gorge.

guests.character: Milhouse Van Houten / Rod Flanders / Lewis (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets flipped backwards through the wall.
episodes.notes: Homer's famous pratfall down Springfield Gorge, bouncing off rocks repeatedly, became one of the series' most memorable visual gags and was later recycled as a flashback in multiple episodes.

episodes.title: Itchy & Scratchy & Marge
episodes.description: Maggie attacks Homer with a mallet after seeing it on Itchy & Scratchy. Marge begins a protest outside the production offices, demanding the show be retooled to prevent further violence.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets squeezed together like a accordion.
episodes.notes: This episode marked the first time The Simpsons directly addressed its own critics who claimed the show promoted violence, making it one of the earliest examples of meta-commentary in the series.

episodes.title: Bart Gets Hit by a Car
episodes.description: After Mr. Burns hits Bart with his car, ambulance-chasing attorney Lionel Hutz persuades Homer and Marge to sue for a million dollars.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions.
episodes.notes: First appearance of ambulance-chasing lawyer Lionel Hutz, voiced by Phil Hartman, who became one of the show's most beloved recurring characters until Hartman's death in 1998.

episodes.title: One Fish, Two Fish, Blowfish, Blue Fish
episodes.description: After eating poisonous blowfish at a sushi restaurant, Homer decides to live life to the fullest in the 24 hours he has left to live.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets squeezed together like a compressed spring before popping back to normal size.
episodes.notes: This episode marked the first time The Simpsons tackled mortality as a central theme, establishing the template for Homer's philosophical growth episodes that would become a series staple.

episodes.title: The Way We Was
episodes.description: When the TV breaks, Marge and Homer entertain the kids with the story of how they first met.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions.
episodes.notes: This is the first flashback episode in Simpsons history, establishing the template for future origin stories and revealing Homer and Marge met in detention at Springfield High School in 1974.

episodes.title: Homer vs. Lisa and the 8th Commandment
episodes.description: Homer becomes the most popular guy in town when he gets an illegal cable hook-up, but Lisa fears he will go to hell for violating the Eighth Commandment.

guests.character: Milhouse Van Houten / Martin Prince / Additional Voices (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions.
episodes.notes: This episode marked the first time The Simpsons directly addressed religious morality as a central plot, establishing the template for later faith-based episodes. It also introduced the recurring gag of Homer's selective interpretation of rules and commandments that would become a defining character trait

episodes.title: Principal Charming
episodes.description: When Marge begs Homer to find Selma a husband, he sets her up with Principal Skinner... who falls in love with Patty instead.

guests.character: Milhouse Van Houten / Lewis / Australian Actress (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets flipped upside down.
episodes.notes: This episode marks the first major romantic storyline for Principal Skinner, establishing his awkward dating persona that would become a recurring character trait throughout the series.

episodes.title: Oh Brother, Where Art Thou?
episodes.description: Homer's long-lost half-brother is the CEO of a car company that's wildly successful... until he hires Homer to help design a car for the average man.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions.
episodes.notes: Introduces Herb Powell, Homer's half-brother voiced by Danny DeVito, in what would become one of only two appearances by this significant family member. The episode's concept of the disastrous "Homer car" became a recurring reference point for spectacular failure throughout the series.

episodes.title: Bart's Dog Gets an 'F'
episodes.description: After a series of destructive acts, Homer decides that Santa's Little Helper must go unless he passes obedience school. As the dog's last hours with the family approach, Bart must find a way to get through to him.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets bounced off when it turns into a bucking bronco.
episodes.notes: This episode marks the first time Santa's Little Helper receives major character development and storyline focus, establishing the template for future pet-centric episodes in the series.

episodes.title: Old Money
episodes.description: When Grandpa's new girlfriend at the Springfield Retirement Castle dies, he inherits her savings and must decide the best way to spend his windfall.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets flipped upside down as the couch rotates.
episodes.notes: First episode to deeply explore Grandpa Simpson's romantic life and establish him as a fully realized character beyond comic relief, featuring the memorable Beatrice Simmons who dies tragically after Grandpa chooses a family outing over spending time with her.

episodes.title: Brush with Greatness
episodes.description: Marge rediscovers her interest in art and takes up painting. When Mr. Burns hires her to paint his official portrait, Marge tries but fails to find his "inner beauty."
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets flipped upside down along with the couch.
episodes.notes: This episode marks the first appearance of Ringo Starr, who voiced himself and provided one of the series' most memorable celebrity cameos by responding to Marge's fan letter from decades earlier.

episodes.title: Lisa's Substitute
episodes.description: With Miss Hoover out sick, a substitute teacher takes the class and becomes an inspiring role model for Lisa.

guests.character: Martin Prince / Wendell / Sherri / Terri / Adrian (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions.
episodes.notes: Dustin Hoffman guest starred as substitute teacher Mr. Bergstrom under the pseudonym "Sam Etic" — a play on "Semitic" — and personally sang "Happy Birthday Lisa" in the episode.

episodes.title: The War of the Simpsons
episodes.description: At a lakeside retreat for married couples, Marge is determined to fix her marriage with Homer, but Homer is more determined to catch General Sherman, the freakishly large catfish that lives in the lake.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.notes: This episode established the recurring gag of Homer's obsession with catching impossibly large fish, a character trait that would resurface throughout the series. The lakeside marriage retreat setting became a template for later episodes exploring marital dysfunction through physical separation from Springfield.

episodes.title: Three Men and a Comic Book
episodes.description: Bart, Milhouse, and Martin pool their money to buy the new first issue of Radioactive Man, but their investment and their friendship are threatened when they can't agree on who will keep it.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets squeezed together like an accordion.
episodes.parody_of: The Treasure of the Sierra Madre
episodes.notes: This episode marks the first appearance of Comic Book Guy, who would become one of the show's most enduring recurring characters with his catchphrase "Worst. Episode. Ever.

episodes.title: Blood Feud
episodes.description: When Mr. Burns is saved by a transfusion of Bart's blood, Homer expects a handsome reward but is outraged to receive only a simple thank-you note.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets flipped upside down along with the couch.
episodes.notes: This episode marked the first time The Simpsons aired outside its regular season, becoming the show's first summer episode and establishing the precedent for occasional off-season specials.

episodes.title: Stark Raving Dad
episodes.description: A deviation from his norm lands Homer in a mental institution, where he shares a room with a man who thinks he's Michael Jackson.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets crushed by a foot from Monty Python's Flying Circus.
episodes.notes: Michael Jackson provided the speaking voice for the character of Michael Jackson but had a sound-alike singer perform all the musical numbers, making this one of the most unusual celebrity guest appearances in TV history.

episodes.title: Mr. Lisa Goes to Washington
episodes.description: Lisa's essay wins the Simpsons a trip to Washington, D.C., where her faith in democracy is shaken by her unethical Congressman.

guests.character: Minnesota Girl / Maria Dominguez / Paper Boy (voice)
Bob Arnold's Secretary / Woman #1 at Lincoln Memorial (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions.
episodes.notes: This episode features the first appearance of Bob Arnold, the corrupt congressman whose bribery scheme Lisa exposes, voiced by Harry Shearer in one of his most memorable one-off political characters.

episodes.title: When Flanders Failed
episodes.description: Flanders suffers a failure after Homer wishes him ill.

guests.character: Rod Flanders / Girl Playing Tag / Homemaker / Dolph (voice)
Maude Flanders / Ned's Friend #1 / Clumsy Woman (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions.
episodes.notes: This episode marks the first time Homer's selfishness and schadenfreude directly causes serious harm to a neighbor, establishing a darker moral complexity that would define later seasons.

episodes.title: Bart the Murderer
episodes.description: While working for Fat Tony, Bart gains enough influence with his boss to solve his problems with Principal Skinner--perhaps permanently.

guests.character: Troy McClure / Joey / Godfather / Lionel Hutz (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets flipped upside down.
episodes.notes: First appearance of Fat Tony, who became the show's primary mob boss character and appeared in over 30 episodes throughout the series

episodes.title: Homer Defined
episodes.description: Homer's averting of a nuclear meltdown earns him employee-of-the-month honors--and a call from Magic Johnson; Milhouse's parents prohibit him from playing with Bart.

guests.character: Milhouse Van Houten / Shelbyville Plant Computer Voice (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets bounced high into the air.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced the term "to pull a Homer" meaning to succeed despite incompetence, which became workplace slang and marked one of the first times a Simpsons phrase entered real-world usage.

episodes.title: Like Father, Like Clown
episodes.description: Bart and Lisa use theological arguments to try to reunite Krusty the Clown and his rabbi father, who disowned Krusty when he turned from rabbi to clown.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets crushed by a large foot in a Monty Python-style stomp.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced Krusty's real name as Herschel Shmoikel Pinchas Yerucham Krustofsky and established his backstory as the son of a rabbi, becoming central to his character mythology.

episodes.title: Treehouse of Horror II
episodes.description: Maggie uses one of three wishes; a mind-reading Bart rules Springfield; Homer donates his brain to Mr. Burns' science project.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets electrocuted, turning into skeletons momentarily before returning to normal.
episodes.parody_of: Horror anthology stories including 'The Monkey's Paw', 'It's a Good Life' (Twilight Zone), and Frankenstein
episodes.notes: This was the first Treehouse of Horror episode to become an annual Halloween tradition, establishing the anthology format that would define the series' most celebrated episodes for over three decades.

episodes.title: Lisa's Pony
episodes.description: After disappointing Lisa, Homer buys her a pony, solving one problem, but creating others, requiring a second job--at Apu's Kwik-E-Mart.

guests.character: Milhouse Van Houten / Credit Union Clerk / Kid (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch normally, then the couch and family sink into the floor.
episodes.notes: Homer's exhaustion-fueled delirium at the Kwik-E-Mart, where he mistakes a customer for a talking dog, became one of the series' most quoted moments about sleep deprivation.

episodes.title: Saturdays of Thunder
episodes.description: When Homer helps Bart build a soapbox racer, it does little for the car but a lot for their relationship.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets crushed by a giant foot.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced the famous "bad father" test that Homer fails spectacularly, establishing a recurring theme of Homer's parental inadequacies that would define countless future episodes. Troy McClure appears in an infomercial for the Juice Loosener

episodes.title: Flaming Moe's
episodes.description: Moe steals the drink recipe Homer invented, and the rechristened “Flaming Moe” turns his tavern into a hotspot. When a national franchise wants to buy the recipe, Homer wants revenge.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets squished together like an accordion.
episodes.notes: The episode's theme song "Where Everybody Knows Your Name" parody became one of the show's most elaborate musical sequences, directly spoofing Cheers down to the opening credits format. This marked the first time The Simpsons devoted an entire episode to parodying a single TV series rather than movies or general concepts.

episodes.title: Burns Verkaufen der Kraftwerk
episodes.description: Burns sells the power plant to a German corporation that doesn't care for Homer's approach to his work.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.notes: This episode marked the first time The Simpsons used a foreign language in its title, with the German phrase meaning "Burns Sells the Power Plant.

episodes.title: I Married Marge
episodes.description: A trip down memory lane to the early days of Simpson wedded bliss, when Marge was with child and Homer was without a job.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions, then spit back out.
episodes.notes: This episode established the canonical timeline that Homer and Marge were married in 1980, with Bart born shortly after, making him a product of shotgun wedding circumstances that would define the Simpson family origin story.

episodes.title: Radio Bart
episodes.description: Bart fools the town into thinking there's a little boy trapped in a well.

guests.character: Milhouse Van Houten / Rod Flanders / Janey Powell (voice)
Martin Prince / Singing Kid in Commercial (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.notes: The "We Are the World" parody "We're Sending Our Love Down the Well" became one of the series' most memorable musical moments, featuring nearly the entire voice cast. This episode established the template for later Springfield-wide media frenzies and hoaxes that would become a recurring theme throughout the series.

episodes.title: Lisa the Greek
episodes.description: Lisa's ability to pick winning football teams kicks off a strong bond with Homer.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets flipped upside down.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced the recurring character of Smooth Jimmy Apollo, the local TV sports prognosticator whose confident predictions are consistently wrong

episodes.title: Homer Alone
episodes.description: A stressed Marge vacations alone, leaving Bart and Lisa with Patty and Selma, and Maggie with Homer--until she leaves in search of Marge.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch normally, but then the couch tips backward and they all fall behind it.
episodes.notes: This episode marks the first time The Simpsons focused primarily on Marge's psychological well-being, pioneering the show's exploration of realistic family stress and mental health issues

episodes.title: Bart the Lover
episodes.description: Bart's answer to detention from Mrs. Krabappel is to answer her personal ad with letters from a Mr. Wonderful, but his last laugh is more of a guilty whimper.

guests.character: Milhouse Van Houten / Rod Flanders / Sparkle (voice)
Miss Hoover / Maude Flanders / Helen Lovejoy (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch normally, but then the couch tips backward and they fall through the floor.
episodes.notes: Introduced the phrase "Ha ha!" as Nelson's signature taunt, which became one of the most recognizable catchphrases in the series.

episodes.title: Homer at the Bat
episodes.description: Burns hires Major League ringers for the plant softball championship.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets flipped upside down, hanging from the ceiling.
episodes.notes: Nine MLB Hall of Famers guest starred as themselves, making it one of the most star-studded episodes in animated TV history. The episode's freak accidents befalling each ringer became a legendary running gag that perfectly spoofed sports movie tropes.

episodes.title: Separate Vocations
episodes.description: The results from their aptitude tests cause Bart and Lisa to make 180-degree turns in their behavior.

guests.character: Milhouse Van Houten / Jimbo Jones / Janey Powell / Additional Voices (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets squished together into a accordion-like cube.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced the character of Snake Jailbird, who would become the show's most frequently appearing criminal and a Springfield staple for decades.

episodes.title: Dog of Death
episodes.description: Santa's Little Helper notices resentment after the Simpsons make sacrifices to fund his life-saving operation.

guests.character: Santa's Little Helper / Mr. Teeny / Hounds (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions.
episodes.notes: This episode features one of the darkest moments in Simpsons history when Santa's Little Helper runs away and joins Mr. Burns' pack of attack dogs, leading to a genuinely tense confrontation where the dog nearly kills Bart.

episodes.title: Colonel Homer
episodes.description: Marge fears a cheatin' heart when a waitress-country singer asks Homer to be her manager.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets crushed by a giant foot in a Monty Python-style stomp.
episodes.parody_of: Country music industry and honky-tonk culture
episodes.notes: Features Beverly D'Angelo as Lurleen Lumpkin, whose song "Your Wife Don't Understand You" became one of the series' most memorable musical numbers and spawned real-world cover versions.

episodes.title: Black Widower
episodes.description: Bart suspects something rotten when Selma gets engaged to Sideshow Bob.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets flipped upside down into a secret lair below.
episodes.parody_of: Film noir detective stories and murder mystery genres
episodes.notes: Kelsey Grammer's second appearance as Sideshow Bob established the character's signature sophisticated villainy and obsession with high culture. This episode pioneered the series' film noir parody style that would become a recurring template for later mystery episodes.

episodes.title: The Otto Show
episodes.description: Spinal Tap hits Springfield, and Otto the bus driver hits the skids after being fired, taking refuge in the Simpsons' garage.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets folded into it as it converts into a bed.
episodes.notes: This marked the first time The Simpsons secured a major rock band to play themselves, with Spinal Tap appearing as fictional characters from their mockumentary film rather than as their real-life alter egos.

episodes.title: Bart's Friend Falls in Love
episodes.description: Bart resents Milhouse's new girlfriend; Homer gets the wrong subliminal tape, which has him gaining vocabulary, not losing weight.

guests.character: Luann Van Houten / Operator / Singing Nun (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets bounced off when it turns into a monster and roars at them.
episodes.notes: First appearance of Milhouse's girlfriend Samantha Stanky, who becomes a recurring character reference despite only appearing in this single episode

episodes.title: Brother, Can You Spare Two Dimes?
episodes.description: Homer's half-brother asks for a loan after Homer is awarded some cash.

guests.character: Maude Flanders / Kristin Shepard / Additional Voices (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets bounced off when an obese man sits down.
episodes.notes: This episode won the Emmy Award for Outstanding Animated Program, making it the first Simpsons episode to receive this honor.

episodes.title: Kamp Krusty
episodes.description: Bart leads an uprising at Krusty's summer camp after he and Lisa are subjected to a Dickensian sweatshop.

guests.character: Miss Hoover / School Nurse / Queen Elizabeth (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets crushed flat by a giant foot.
episodes.notes: This season premiere marked the first time The Simpsons opened a season with a non-Halloween special, breaking from the show's early tradition of starting seasons with "Treehouse of Horror" episodes.

episodes.title: A Streetcar Named Marge
episodes.description: Marge is cast in a version of "A Streetcar Named Desire," playing Blanche to Ned Flanders' Stanley.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets shuffled like a deck of cards.
episodes.notes: This episode features the debut of Ayn Rand School for Tots, where Maggie is traumatized by the daycare's dystopian rule that all items belong to everyone. The "Oh Streetcar!" musical number became one of the series' most elaborate Broadway-style parodies, complete with full choreography and a cast of dozens

episodes.title: Homer the Heretic
episodes.description: After skipping church one Sunday morning, Homer decides to quit church altogether and spend Sundays worshiping in his own way -- with cigars and beer.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets crushed by a giant foot.
episodes.notes: God speaks directly to Homer in this episode, voiced by Harry Shearer using his normal speaking voice rather than any of his character voices. This marks one of the few times a divine entity appears as an actual character in the series, establishing the show's unusual approach to religious themes.

episodes.title: Lisa the Beauty Queen
episodes.description: Homer enters Lisa into the Little Miss Springfield Pageant to help her feel better about her looks. But when she must choose between the title and her integrity, she realizes that confidence comes from within.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions, then ejected back out.
episodes.notes: This episode features the first appearance of Amber Dempsey, who beats Lisa by performing a flaming baton routine that would become one of the most memorable pageant moments in animated television.

episodes.title: Treehouse of Horror III
episodes.description: A Halloween trilogy includes a murderous Krusty doll; a "King Kong" parody; an invasion of zombies unleashed by Bart's incantation.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets electrocuted, turning into skeletons briefly before returning to normal.
episodes.parody_of: King Kong, Night Gallery, Pet Sematary
episodes.notes: The Krusty doll segment introduced the now-iconic line "It's set to evil!" when Grandpa Simpson discovers the toy's good/evil switch. This episode marked the first time a Treehouse of Horror segment directly parodied a specific film (King Kong), establishing the anthology's template for movie spoofs.

episodes.title: Itchy & Scratchy: The Movie
episodes.description: Bart is punished by being barred from the Itchy and Scratchy movie.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets crushed flat by a giant foot.
episodes.notes: This episode features the iconic flash-forward ending where an elderly Bart finally sees the Itchy & Scratchy movie with his own son, establishing one of the series' most memorable glimpses into the characters' future lives.

episodes.title: Marge Gets a Job
episodes.description: Mr. Burns is instantly smitten when Marge takes a job at the nuclear power plant.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.notes: The King Kong parody finale features one of the most elaborate and expensive sequences in early Simpsons history, with Burns climbing Springfield's tallest building while clutching Marge

episodes.title: New Kid on the Block
episodes.description: Bart has a crush on his new neighbor, a teenage girl who only has eyes for Jimbo Jones. Bart sets out to prove that Jimbo is not as tough as he seems.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions.
episodes.notes: This episode introduces Laura Powers, Bart's first major crush, voiced by Sara Gilbert in her only Simpsons appearance. The storyline established the template for Bart's future romantic episodes and his ongoing rivalry with the bullies.

episodes.title: Mr. Plow
episodes.description: Homer and Barney become competitors in the snow-plowing business.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets flipped upside down when the couch turns over.
episodes.notes: The Mr. Plow jingle became one of the series' most memorable commercial parodies, with Homer's snow plow business card design inspiring countless fan recreations and merchandise

episodes.title: Lisa's First Word
episodes.description: While trying to get Maggie to say her first word, Marge and Homer reminisce about Bart's and Lisa's first words.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets bounced off when it turns into a monster and growls at them.
episodes.notes: This episode revealed that Maggie's first word was "Daddy," spoken only to Homer in a tender closing scene that became one of the series' most touching moments.

episodes.title: Homer's Triple Bypass
episodes.description: Homer sees a discount doctor for a coronary bypass.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets crushed by a giant foot.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced Dr. Nick Riviera with his iconic catchphrase "Hi everybody!" — he would become a recurring character appearing in over 20 episodes.

episodes.title: Marge vs. the Monorail
episodes.description: When Springfield gets a sudden windfall, a charismatic traveling salesman convinces the town to build a monorail.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets squeezed together like a accordion, then springs back to normal size.
episodes.notes: Written by Conan O'Brien during his time as a writer on the show, this episode's "Monorail Song" became one of the most frequently quoted musical numbers in Simpsons history and helped establish O'Brien's reputation before he transitioned to late-night television.

episodes.title: Selma's Choice
episodes.description: After seeing Aunt Gladys die alone, Aunt Selma wants to get married and have a baby... until she spends a day babysitting Bart and Lisa at Duff Gardens.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets shuffled around like a deck of cards.
episodes.notes: First appearance of Duff Gardens, the show's brilliant parody of Disneyland that became one of the most memorable fictional theme parks in television history

episodes.title: Brother from the Same Planet
episodes.description: When Homer neglects Bart once too often, Bart goes to the Big Brother program to get a new father figure. Stung, Homer replaces Bart with a little brother.

guests.character: Tom / Nelson's Dad / Football Commentator / Announcer (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions, then shot out through the back.
episodes.notes: Tom, Bart's Big Brother, is voiced by Phil Hartman in what many consider one of his most memorable Simpsons performances, showcasing his ability to play both charming and subtly sinister characters.

episodes.title: I Love Lisa
episodes.description: Chief Wiggum's son has a crush on Lisa after she gives him a Valentine out of pity.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets flipped upside down along with the couch.
episodes.notes: Features Ralph Wiggum's first major speaking role and introduced his iconic childlike malapropisms, including "I choo-choo-choose you" which became one of the series' most quoted Valentine's Day references.

episodes.title: Duffless
episodes.description: After a DUI, Homer swears off beer for a month. Meanwhile, Bart and Lisa's sibling rivalry plays out in the school science fair.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets bounced off when it turns into a bucking bronco.
episodes.notes: Features the series' most extensive parody of A Clockwork Orange, with Homer's aversion therapy sequence directly mirroring Stanley Kubrick's controversial film techniques

episodes.title: Last Exit to Springfield
episodes.description: Union president Homer fights to keep the dental plan.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.parody_of: Norma Rae and various labor union films
episodes.notes: Often cited as the greatest Simpsons episode ever made, this labor union satire features Homer's iconic "dental plan/Lisa needs braces" internal monologue that became one of the series' most quoted moments.

episodes.title: So It's Come to This: A Simpsons Clip Show
episodes.description: An April Fools' Day prank provides the jumping-off point for Simpson family flashbacks.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch normally.
episodes.notes: The Simpsons' first clip show aired on April Fools' Day as a meta-joke on audiences expecting new content, with the prank setup serving as the thinnest possible framework for recycling old footage.

episodes.title: The Front
episodes.description: Lisa and Bart write an Itchy and Scratchy episode; Homer makes up a missing high-school credit.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets launched through the ceiling.
episodes.notes: This episode features the first appearance of Roger Meyers Jr., the sleazy head of Itchy & Scratchy Studios, who would become a recurring character representing Hollywood executives throughout the series.

episodes.title: Whacking Day
episodes.description: Lisa takes a stand against Springfield's annual snake-bashing festival.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets squeezed together like an accordion.
episodes.notes: Barry White guest-starred as himself, lending his distinctive bass voice to help Lisa and Bart use music to lure the snakes to safety.

episodes.title: Marge in Chains
episodes.description: Overwhelmed by her family's demands during a flu epidemic, Marge accidentally shoplifts at the Kwik-E-Mart and winds up in prison.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.notes: Features the first appearance of Lionel Hutz as Marge's incompetent lawyer, with Phil Hartman's memorable performance launching the character into eight more episodes before Hartman's death in 1998

episodes.title: Krusty Gets Kancelled
episodes.description: Krusty's Hollywood pals help when his show is canceled after a new star causes his ratings to plummet.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets folded into the couch which turns into a pull-out bed.
episodes.parody_of: The Tonight Show and variety show format
episodes.notes: Features guest appearances by Johnny Carson, Bette Midler, Hugh Hefner, and the Red Hot Chili Peppers as themselves, marking one of the highest concentrations of celebrity cameos in the series at that time.

episodes.title: Homer's Barbershop Quartet
episodes.description: Homer recalls when he, Apu, Barney and Principal Skinner became recording icons.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions.
episodes.notes: This episode features one of the most elaborate Beatles parodies in TV history, with the Be Sharps storyline directly mirroring the Fab Four's rise and fall complete with rooftop concert and Yoko-inspired breakup.

episodes.title: Cape Feare
episodes.description: The Simpsons go incognito to avoid the newly paroled Sideshow Bob, who's out to get Bart.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which tips backward and launches them through the wall.
episodes.notes: Kelsey Grammer's haunting rendition of the entire score from HMS Pinafore was added to pad the episode's runtime, creating one of the series' most memorable musical sequences.

episodes.title: Homer Goes to College
episodes.description: Homer takes Nuclear Physics 101 at the local college, where he gets a trio of nerdy tutors expelled for extracurricular activities.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets flipped upside down as the couch rotates.
episodes.parody_of: College films like Animal House and Revenge of the Nerds
episodes.notes: This episode introduced the recurring characters of Benjamin, Doug, and Gary, the nerdy college students who would appear in several future episodes as Homer's academic associates.

episodes.title: Rosebud
episodes.description: Burns longs for a childhood toy that winds up in the grip of Maggie.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets flipped upside down into a money slot like a vending machine.
episodes.notes: This episode features the first appearance of Mr. Burns' childhood teddy bear Bobo, which would become a recurring element in later episodes and tie into the show's mythology about Burns' past.

episodes.title: Treehouse of Horror IV
episodes.description: Homer sells his soul; Vampire Burns invites the Simpsons for dinner; Bart sees a gremlin damaging his school bus.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch as skeletons, then their flesh grows back onto their bones.
episodes.parody_of: Multiple horror films including Twilight Zone, Dracula, and classic monster movies
episodes.notes: This episode introduced the iconic "Nightmare on Elm Street" parody with the killer school bus driver Otto, and featured the first appearance of vampire Mr. Burns in what became one of the most memorable Treehouse of Horror segments.

episodes.title: Marge on the Lam
episodes.description: Marge's wild night out with neighbor Ruth includes running from the police in Ruth's car, stolen from her ex.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets crushed flat by a giant foot from above.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced Ruth Powers, voiced by Pamela Reed, who became Marge's recurring friend and confidante in several future episodes. The Thelma & Louise parody ends with Homer and Chief Wiggum recreating the film's famous cliff scene, but with characteristically anti-climactic Simpsons humor.

episodes.title: Bart's Inner Child
episodes.description: Springfield emulates Bart after he's praised by a self-help guru.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions, then shot out through the back.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced self-help guru Brad Goodman, voiced by Albert Brooks in his second of three guest appearances on the series. The "Do What You Feel" festival disaster became a template for Springfield's later town-wide crazes and mob mentality episodes

episodes.title: Boy-Scoutz 'N the Hood
episodes.description: After a reckless Squishee bender with Milhouse, Bart wakes to the shocking realization that he joined the Junior Campers. Lured by pocketknives and knot-tying lessons, Bart assimilates into the group but then must face the father-son rafting trip.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which folds into a bed with them on it.
episodes.notes: Features Ernest Borgnine as himself leading the Junior Campers, in one of the most memorable celebrity guest appearances where the actor plays an exaggerated version of his tough-guy persona.

episodes.title: The Last Temptation of Homer
episodes.description: Homer struggles with his attraction to new coworker Mindy, a beautiful engineer whose love for doughnuts is equal to his own.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one until only their eyes are visible.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced Mindy Simmons, voiced by Michelle Pfeiffer, who was chosen specifically because the writers wanted someone whose voice would genuinely tempt Homer away from Marge.

episodes.title: $pringfield (Or, How I Learned to Stop Worrying and Love Legalized Gambling)
episodes.description: Legalized gambling comes to Springfield, and Marge becomes addicted; Robert Goulet entertains at Bart's casino for kids.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets shuffled like a deck of cards.
episodes.notes: This episode marks the first time a Simpsons character develops a serious addiction, with Marge's gambling problem treated with unprecedented psychological realism for the series.

episodes.title: Homer the Vigilante
episodes.description: Homer leads a vigilante group to catch the Springfield Cat Burglar, who then robs the museum.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets crushed by a foot from Monty Python's Flying Circus.
episodes.notes: This episode features the first appearance of the Cat Burglar, voiced by Sam Neill, who delivers one of the series' most memorable villain monologues about the "World's Greatest Grandfather" fake artifact.

episodes.title: Bart Gets Famous
episodes.description: When an on-air remark catapults Bart to instant stardom, he finds that fame isn't all it's cracked up to be.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which tips backward and launches them through the wall and into the night sky.
episodes.notes: Bart's catchphrase "I didn't do it" from this episode became a genuine cultural phenomenon, inspiring real-world merchandise and entering the popular lexicon alongside "D'oh!" The episode marked one of the first times The Simpsons directly examined celebrity culture and the fleeting nature of fame.

episodes.title: Homer and Apu
episodes.description: Homer accompanies Apu to the Kwik-E-Mart home office--in India--after Apu is fired.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the TV, where they appear as characters in various TV shows.
episodes.notes: This episode features the first extended international setting in The Simpsons, taking Homer and Apu to India in a storyline that required extensive research into Indian culture and customs by the writing staff.

episodes.title: Lisa vs. Malibu Stacy
episodes.description: Lisa's outrage over the gender stereotypes reinforced by a new talking doll prompts her to confront the dollmaker.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets folded into it like a Hide-A-Bed.
episodes.parody_of: Barbie dolls and toy industry gender stereotypes
episodes.notes: Features the debut of Smithers' beloved Malibu Stacy creator Stacy Lovell, voiced by Kathleen Turner in a rare animated guest appearance. The episode's feminist themes made it groundbreaking for its direct critique of toy industry gender stereotypes, predating similar mainstream discussions by years.

episodes.title: Deep Space Homer
episodes.description: Homer and Barney compete to be the first "average" American in space.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets flipped upside down as if they're on a rotating carnival ride.
episodes.parody_of: The Right Stuff and NASA space program
episodes.notes: This episode features the iconic moment where Homer floats past the window eating potato chips in zero gravity, which NASA later cited as one of the most accurate depictions of eating in space on television.

episodes.title: Homer Loves Flanders
episodes.description: Flanders' extra ticket to the big game makes him Homer's new best friend, a role that sorely tests Flanders' patience.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets bounced high into the air, crashing through the ceiling.
episodes.notes: This episode marks the rare instance where Homer genuinely likes Ned Flanders, creating one of the series' most psychologically complex character reversals that deeply unsettles both characters and viewers alike.

episodes.title: Bart Gets an Elephant
episodes.description: Bart wins an elephant, but Homer won't let him keep it.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets bounced high into the air.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced Stampy the elephant, who became one of the show's most memorable one-off characters and inspired countless fan theories about his fate after being sent to live with other elephants.

episodes.title: Burns' Heir
episodes.description: Bart is picked to be Burns' heir and moves into the Burns mansion, prompting Homer and Marge to hire a deprogrammer to get him back.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets bounced off when it turns into a bucking bronco.
episodes.notes: Features the debut of the "Egg Council Guy," a mysterious corporate figure who became an enduring internet meme despite appearing for only seconds.

episodes.title: Sweet Seymour Skinner's Baadasssss Song
episodes.description: Bart takes his dog to school, which gets Skinner fired, which in turn leaves Bart missing his archnemesis.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.notes: This episode marks the first time Bart genuinely misses Principal Skinner, establishing their oddly symbiotic relationship that would become a recurring theme throughout the series.

episodes.title: The Boy Who Knew Too Much
episodes.description: Bart can clear Mayor Quimby's nephew of a charge of battery, but only by implicating himself as a hooky player.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets squeezed through the back like toothpaste, coming out as a single long string.
episodes.notes: This episode features one of the most faithful parodies in Simpsons history, recreating entire scenes and dialogue from Sidney Lumet's "12 Angry Men" with remarkable attention to detail.

episodes.title: Lady Bouvier's Lover
episodes.description: Grandpa Simpson falls for Marge's mother, but loses out to a competitor; Bart gets ripped off shopping at home with Homer's credit card.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one until only their eyes are visible.
episodes.notes: This episode introduces Mr. Burns' romantic rival for Jackie Bouvier's affections, marking one of the few times Burns shows genuine vulnerability in matters of the heart

episodes.title: Secrets of a Successful Marriage
episodes.description: Homer teaches a class on marriage, but sharing secrets about his own union gets him kicked out of the house.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions like quicksand.
episodes.notes: This episode features Homer's memorable declaration "I am so smart! S-M-R-T!" while spelling out "smart" incorrectly, which became one of the series' most quoted malapropisms.

episodes.title: Bart of Darkness
episodes.description: Stuck in his room with a broken leg, Bart hears a high-pitched scream. When he looks through his telescope, he sees a guilty Ned Flanders burying the evidence.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.notes: This episode marked the first time The Simpsons directly parodied a specific Alfred Hitchcock film, launching a tradition of classic movie homages that would become a series hallmark.

episodes.title: Lisa's Rival
episodes.description: Lisa feels threatened when a new girl moves to Springfield: Allison is smart, pretty, a great saxophone player and a crack diorama builder. Lisa tries to befriend her new rival, but she's consumed with envy and competitiveness.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.notes: First appearance of Allison Taylor, voiced by Winona Ryder at the height of her 1990s fame, marking one of the show's most high-profile guest stars to play a child character.

episodes.title: Another Simpsons Clip Show
episodes.description: After reading the weepy best-seller The Bridges of Madison County, Marge sits down with the family and recalls romantic moments, along with temptations from her bowling instructor Jacques and Homer's coworker Mindy.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch normally, but then the couch and family slide backward through the wall.
episodes.notes: This marked the second clip show in the series' history, making The Simpsons one of the first animated series to regularly use the cost-saving format that would become standard practice across television

episodes.title: Itchy & Scratchy Land
episodes.description: The family's trip to Itchy & Scratchy Land takes an unexpected turn when high-tech robots malfunction and become violent.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch which turns into a monster and swallows them.
episodes.notes: This episode marked the first time The Simpsons directly parodied Michael Crichton's work, combining elements from both Westworld and Jurassic Park in a single story. The malfunctioning robot uprising plot would later influence numerous other animated series attempting similar theme park disaster episodes.

episodes.title: Sideshow Bob Roberts
episodes.description: Mayor Quimby pardons attempted murderer Sideshow Bob, who then runs against Quimby for mayor.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets flipped upside down, with the room rotating around them.
episodes.parody_of: Political thrillers and election fraud films, particularly 'All the President's Men'
episodes.notes: Kelsey Grammer's second appearance as Sideshow Bob introduced his campaign song "We Are the Mediocre Presidents," which became one of the series' most memorable musical moments despite the episode not being classified as a musical episode.

episodes.title: Treehouse of Horror V
episodes.description: Lack of beer and cable TV make Homer a crazed killer; the present is altered by a time-traveling Homer; and Principal Skinner turns detention students into food.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the floor by quicksand.
episodes.parody_of: The Shining, A Sound of Thunder/time travel stories, Soylent Green
episodes.notes: This episode features the iconic "No TV and no beer make Homer go crazy" sequence, which became one of the most quoted and parodied moments in Simpsons history.

episodes.title: Bart's Girlfriend
episodes.description: Bart learns that the good Reverend Lovejoy's daughter is really bad.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets flipped upside down as the couch rotates.
episodes.notes: Introduces Jessica Lovejoy, voiced by Meryl Streep, as Reverend Lovejoy's rebellious daughter who becomes Bart's first serious love interest. This marks one of the most prestigious guest voice performances in the series' early seasons.

episodes.title: Lisa on Ice
episodes.description: Lisa's surprising ability at hockey spawns jealousy in Bart and a showdown when their teams meet.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets catapulted through the ceiling when the couch turns into a catapult.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced the phrase "That's my sister out there!" followed by "That's my brother!" — the first time Bart and Lisa's sibling rivalry transformed into mutual protection, establishing a template the show would return to repeatedly in future seasons.

episodes.title: Homer Badman
episodes.description: When Homer gropes for a gummy candy stuck to the babysitter's pants, she mistakes his intentions and turns the town against him.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch normally, but then the couch and family are revealed to be a painting that gets hung in a museum.
episodes.parody_of: Media sensationalism and tabloid television shows
episodes.notes: This episode introduced the character of Ashley Grant, the babysitter whose false accusation drives the plot, and became one of the show's most prescient satires of media-driven moral panics that would dominate the following decades.

episodes.title: Grampa vs. Sexual Inadequacy
episodes.description: Grandpa's homemade tonic puts life back in the Simpson bedroom, so he and Homer hit the road to sell the stuff.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch normally, but then the couch and family slide backwards through the wall and into the neighbor's house.
episodes.notes: This episode features one of the series' most emotionally complex storylines, exploring the strained relationship between Homer and Grampa while tackling adult themes rarely addressed so directly in the show's early seasons

episodes.title: Fear of Flying
episodes.description: When they try to go on vacation, the family discovers Marge's debilitating fear of flying. Marge consults a psychoanalyst to find the source of her baffling fear.

guests.character: Dr. J. Loren Pryor (voice) (as Doug Hill)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions, then shot out through the ceiling.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced Dr. Zweig, voiced by Anne Bancroft in her only Simpsons appearance, delivering one of the show's most acclaimed guest performances as Marge's psychoanalyst.

episodes.title: Homer the Great
episodes.description: Homer joins the secret society of the Stonecutters and is suddenly exalted when the brethren discover his birthmark. The ancient order is shaken to the core by the ineptitude of its new Chosen One.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets crushed flat by a giant foot in a Monty Python-style animation.
episodes.parody_of: Secret societies like the Freemasons and Illuminati
episodes.notes: The Stonecutters' song "We Do" became one of the series' most memorable musical numbers, with its catchy melody and absurd lyrics about who controls various aspects of society.

episodes.title: And Maggie Makes Three
episodes.description: The story of Maggie's birth is told in flashbacks: after Homer quits the Springfield Nuclear Power Plant to pursue his dream of working at a bowling alley, Marge reveals that she is pregnant.
episodes.couch_gag: The family runs to the couch, but it tips backward and they all fall behind it.
episodes.notes: This episode revealed the heartwarming reason behind Homer's "DO IT FOR HER" photo collage in his work station, which became one of the series' most emotionally resonant visual gags.

episodes.title: Bart's Comet
episodes.description: When Bart is forced to assist Principal Skinner in early-morning astronomy observations, he discovers a giant comet heading towards Springfield. Facing impending doom, the entire town shows up at the Flanders family bomb shelter.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which folds into a bed with the Simpsons tucked in under blankets.
episodes.notes: This episode features one of the series' most memorable background gags when Moe suggests the comet will burn up in the atmosphere, becoming "a few burned-out rocks" — then cut to a massive fireball still hurtling toward Earth. The shelter sequence became iconic for cramming nearly every Springfield resident into one scene, requiring animators to draw an unprecedented number of characters in a single location.

episodes.title: Homie the Clown
episodes.description: Krusty the Clown's reckless spending forces him to open a clown college to keep afloat. New graduate Homer discovers the perks and perils of being Krusty.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which is actually a folded-out sofa bed that closes up on them.
episodes.notes: This episode features one of the most elaborate Goodfellas parodies in television history, with Fat Tony's crew mistaking Homer for Krusty in a sequence that mirrors Henry Hill's paranoid final act from the film.

episodes.title: Bart vs. Australia
episodes.description: After tricking an Australian boy into accepting a collect call, Bart flies Down Under to apologize.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions, then shot out through the back.
episodes.notes: This episode caused a minor diplomatic incident when the Australian government formally complained to Fox about the country's portrayal, making it one of the few Simpsons episodes to prompt an official international response

episodes.title: Homer vs. Patty and Selma
episodes.description: A loan from Patty and Selma makes Homer their slave; Bart is forced to take ballet.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.notes: This episode features one of the series' most memorable sight gags when Patty and Selma force Homer to wear a "World's Greatest Sister-in-Law" apron while doing their housework. The ballet subplot introduced the recurring theme of Bart discovering unexpected talents he's embarrassed about, later revisited in episodes involving his natural ability at golf and other activities.

episodes.title: A Star Is Burns
episodes.description: Film critic Jay Sherman is among the judges at the Springfield Film Festival, which Mr. Burns conspires to win by bribing some judges.

guests.character: Milhouse Van Houten / Rod Flanders / Maude Flanders (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which folds into a bed, then into a suitcase that gets carried away.
episodes.notes: This crossover episode with The Critic was so controversial that Matt Groening removed his name from the credits, calling it a betrayal of The Simpsons' independent creative vision.

episodes.title: Lisa's Wedding
episodes.description: A fortuneteller predicts Lisa's engagement and wedding to a fellow student from an upper-crust British family.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch normally, but then the couch and family slide backward through the wall and end up outside behind the house.
episodes.notes: This episode marked the first future-set episode in Simpsons history, establishing a template that would be revisited multiple times throughout the series. The fortune teller was voiced by actress Maggie Roswell, who also played Maude Flanders and other recurring female characters

episodes.title: Two Dozen and One Greyhounds
episodes.description: The Simpson household's overrun with puppies--the offspring of Santa's Little Helper and his new girlfriend from which Mr. Burns would love to make a greyhound-skin tuxedo.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which turns into a monster and swallows them whole.
episodes.notes: This episode features one of the series' most elaborate musical sequences with "See My Vest," a darkly comic parody of "Be Our Guest" from Beauty and the Beast that showcases Mr. Burns' collection of clothing made from exotic animals.

episodes.title: The PTA Disbands
episodes.description: Bart helps precipitate a teachers strike, but he rues his handiwork when Marge takes over for Mrs. Krabappel.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch which folds into a bed, and they all fall asleep.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced the recurring gag of Purple Monkey Dishwasher, a nonsensical phrase that gets distorted through a chain of whispered communication during the PTA meeting. The teachers' strike storyline became one of the show's most acclaimed examinations of labor disputes and educational dysfunction.

episodes.title: Round Springfield
episodes.description: Bart winds up in the hospital as a result of eating Krusty cereal, and when Lisa comes to visit him, she is reunited with her sax idol.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets crushed flat by a giant foot from Monty Python's Flying Circus.
episodes.notes: Features the only appearance of Bleeding Gums Murphy, Lisa's jazz mentor who dies in this episode, making it one of the series' most emotionally impactful character deaths.

episodes.title: The Springfield Connection
episodes.description: Marge joins the police force after thwarting a criminal, and Homer thinks he now has a free ride as a cop's spouse.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets bounced off by the couch like a trampoline.
episodes.parody_of: Police procedural genre and cop shows
episodes.notes: This episode marks the first time a main Simpson family member becomes a police officer, with Marge's brief law enforcement career providing one of the series' most thorough parodies of cop show tropes.

episodes.title: Lemon of Troy
episodes.description: Springfield boys try to recapture the town's beloved lemon tree after it's stolen.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets bounced up to the ceiling, then falls back down.
episodes.parody_of: The Bridge on the River Kwai
episodes.notes: This episode marks the first time viewers see Shelbyville, Springfield's rival town, establishing the longstanding feud between the two cities that would become a recurring element throughout the series.

episodes.title: Who Shot Mr. Burns? (1)
episodes.description: Mr. Burns makes many enemies when he steals the oil discovered beneath Springfield Elementary.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which folds into a bed with the family inside it.
episodes.notes: The first season-ending cliffhanger in Simpsons history, directly parodying the famous "Who Shot J.R.?" storyline from Dallas that captivated America in 1980.

episodes.title: Who Shot Mr. Burns? (2)
episodes.description: With Mr. Burns shot, Chief Wiggum investigates the crime, and nearly everyone in town has a motive. At the end, it all comes down to the Simpson DNA.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets shot full of holes by a Tommy gun emerging from the wall.
episodes.parody_of: Dallas 'Who Shot J.R.?' storyline and murder mystery genre
episodes.notes: The resolution that Mr. Burns shot himself accidentally while trying to steal candy from a baby became one of the most controversial endings in TV history, with many fans feeling cheated by the anticlimactic reveal. This two-part event generated more viewer complaints to Fox than any previous Simpsons episode.

episodes.title: Radioactive Man
episodes.description: Bart auditions for the part of Fallout Boy in the movie "Radioactive Man," being filmed in Springfield.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the TV, appearing in black and white like an old sitcom.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced the catchphrase "Up and at them!" which Bart repeatedly mispronounces as "Up and atom!" during his stint as Fallout Boy, creating one of the series' most quotable running gags

episodes.title: Home Sweet Homediddly-Dum-Doodily
episodes.description: When Homer and Marge are found to be unfit parents, the kids are placed with the Flanders family. Reluctant participants in the Flanderses' wholesome family activities, Bart and Lisa become the targets of an emergency baptism.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced the Child Protective Services caseworker who would become a recurring character, and featured one of the series' most dramatic cliffhangers with Bart about to be baptized against his will.

episodes.title: Bart Sells His Soul
episodes.description: Bart sells his soul to Milhouse for five dollars, but soon regrets his decision. Meanwhile, Moe turns the Tavern into a family-friendly restaurant.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which deflates like a balloon and sends them flying around the room.
episodes.notes: This episode features the only major storyline where Bart literally sells his soul, creating one of the series' most poignant explorations of spirituality and childhood anxiety about the afterlife.

episodes.title: Lisa the Vegetarian
episodes.description: After visiting a local petting zoo, Lisa becomes a vegetarian and faces ridicule from family and friends. When she lashes out and ruins Homer's barbecue, she learns a lesson in tolerance.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.notes: Paul and Linda McCartney agreed to guest star only on the condition that Lisa would remain a vegetarian for the rest of the series — a promise the writers have kept for nearly three decades

episodes.title: Treehouse of Horror VI
episodes.description: In "Attack of the 50 Foot Eyesores," strange atmospheric conditions bring giant advertising statues to life. In "Nightmare on Evergreen Terrace," Groundskeeper Willie is killed in a freak accident and seeks revenge in the childrens' dreams. In the final segment, Homer steps through a secret portal and becomes three-dimensional.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets pulled underground by a trapdoor, landing in an empty theater where they watch the episode on screen.
episodes.parody_of: A Nightmare on Elm Street, Attack of the 50 Foot Woman, and various monster movies
episodes.notes: The third segment "Homer³" featured groundbreaking computer animation that made Homer the first traditionally animated character to be rendered in 3D, requiring collaboration with Pacific Data Images who would later become part of DreamWorks Animation.

episodes.title: King-Size Homer
episodes.description: Homer tries to gain sixty pounds so he can stay home on disability. When he nearly causes a catastrophe at the plant, Homer reconsiders his plan.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which deflates like a balloon and sends them flying around the room.
episodes.notes: Features Homer's iconic muumuu scene and introduces his catchphrase "I wash myself with a rag on a stick," which became one of the series' most quoted fat jokes.

episodes.title: Mother Simpson
episodes.description: Homer discovers that his mother is still alive, a former radical who is a fugitive from the law. When her old nemesis Burns spots her and tips off the FBI, she is forced to return to the underground.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch which then transforms into a monster that swallows them whole.
episodes.notes: This episode marks the only appearance of Homer's mother Mona Simpson, voiced by Glenn Close, revealing Homer's backstory and why he believed she was dead. The emotional ending, where Homer sits alone on his car watching the stars after his mother leaves again, is considered one of the series' most poignant moments.

episodes.title: Sideshow Bob's Last Gleaming
episodes.description: After Sideshow Bob escapes from prison, the Springfield Air Show is the setting for his ill-fated vendetta against television and Krusty the Clown.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets crushed flat by a giant foot from Monty Python's Flying Circus.
episodes.parody_of: Die Hard and airport thriller films
episodes.notes: Kelsey Grammer's third appearance as Sideshow Bob features his most elaborate scheme yet, establishing the character's recurring obsession with high culture versus lowbrow entertainment. The episode's climactic sequence aboard the stolen Wright Flyer directly parodies the airplane finale from North by Northwest.

episodes.title: The Simpsons 138th Episode Spectacular
episodes.description: Troy McClure hosts a behind-the-scenes look at the show, including clips of the series' beginnings as shorts on "The Tracy Ullman Show," Simpsons trivia questions, and unaired scenes from popular episodes.
episodes.parody_of: Behind-the-scenes TV specials and clip shows
episodes.notes: This clip show became the first Simpsons episode to break the fourth wall by acknowledging the show's production history, including airing actual footage from the original Tracy Ullman Show shorts that launched the series

episodes.title: Marge Be Not Proud
episodes.description: Nabbed for shoplifting, Bart manages to keep it a secret from the family... until it's time to have the family Christmas photo taken at the Try-N-Save.

guests.character: Milhouse Van Houten / Jimbo Jones / Boy in Commercial (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets squished together like accordion bellows.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced the phrase "I didn't do it" as Bart's signature catchphrase, which became so popular that it was later referenced and parodied throughout the series and in popular culture

episodes.title: Team Homer
episodes.description: Homer catches Mr. Burns in an ether-induced haze and asks for $500 for his bowling team. When Burns' head clears, he demands to join the team. Meanwhile, Bart's inflammatory T-shirt provokes Principal Skinner to require school uniforms.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets catapulted through the ceiling.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced the Pin Pals bowling team and features one of the series' most memorable sight gags: Mr. Burns so weakened he can barely knock down bowling pins despite multiple attempts.

episodes.title: Two Bad Neighbors
episodes.description: Homer resents being upstaged when former president George Bush moves into the neighborhood. After Bush spanks Bart, an all-out prank war ensues.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets flipped upside down, with the couch on the ceiling.
episodes.notes: Former President George H.W. Bush voiced himself in this episode, making it one of the rare times a sitting or former U.S. President appeared as themselves on The Simpsons. The episode aired just four years after Bush left office, during a period when he was largely out of the public eye.

episodes.title: Scenes from the Class Struggle in Springfield
episodes.description: A chance encounter with a former classmate yields an invitation to the Springfield Country Club, where Marge tries to parlay a bargain basement Chanel suit into elevated social status for her family.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one until only their eyes are visible.
episodes.notes: This episode marks the first and only appearance of the Ogdenville Country Club set, which required one of the most elaborate background designs in the series' early seasons.

episodes.title: Bart the Fink
episodes.description: In a scheme to get Krusty's autograph, Bart sends him a check. When Krusty's offshore holding corporation endorses the check, his illegal tax shelter is exposed. In despair after the government auctions off his possessions, Krusty fakes his own death.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
