episodes.notes: This episode introduced the concept of Krusty's elaborate financial crimes and tax evasion schemes, which would become a recurring element of his character in later episodes

episodes.title: Lisa the Iconoclast
episodes.description: While researching into Jebediah Springfield's past for an essay, Lisa discovers that the town founder wasn't the hero that everyone thinks he was.

guests.character: Woman in Film / Betsy Ross / Mrs. Glick (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets crushed flat by a giant foot from Monty Python's Flying Circus.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced the character of Hollis Hurlbut, the obsessive town historian voiced by Donald Sutherland, who would become a recurring figure in Springfield's historical episodes

episodes.title: Homer the Smithers
episodes.description: Forced to take a vacation, Smithers needs to find a temporary replacement who won't outshine him. Homer seems the perfect choice until a sudden outburst forces Mr. Burns to assert his independence.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets flipped upside down, with their legs sticking up in the air.
episodes.notes: This episode features one of the series' most memorable role reversals, with Homer's incompetence as Smithers leading to the rare sight of Mr. Burns functioning independently and ultimately rejecting his need for a devoted assistant.

episodes.title: The Day the Violence Died
episodes.description: Bart meets the original animator who created Itchy, penniless some 70 years after the studio stole his idea. When Bart helps him sue for restitution, he unwittingly bankrupts Itchy & Scratchy.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which is actually a sheet of paper that gets crumpled up by a giant hand.
episodes.parody_of: Animation industry history and copyright disputes
episodes.notes: This episode introduced Chester J. Lampwick, voiced by Kirk Douglas in his only Simpsons appearance, as the forgotten creator of Itchy who battles animation giant Roger Meyers Jr. in a landmark copyright lawsuit. The story directly parodies real animation industry disputes, particularly the case of Ub Iwerks versus Walt Disney over Mickey Mouse's creation.

episodes.title: A Fish Called Selma
episodes.description: Washed up and plagued by scandal, Troy McClure sees a marriage to Selma as his ticket back to success.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the floor like quicksand.
episodes.notes: Phil Hartman's Troy McClure delivers his most memorable performance in this episode, which became a fan-favorite showcase for the character just two years before Hartman's tragic death ended the role forever.

episodes.title: Bart on the Road
episodes.description: Bart creates a fake driver's license for himself and uses it to go on a spring break road trip with Milhouse, Nelson, and Martin. Meanwhile, Homer and Lisa bond after spending some quality time together at work.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which is actually a monster that swallows them whole.
episodes.notes: This episode features one of the series' most elaborate B-plots, with Homer and Lisa's nuclear plant subplot running nearly equal time to Bart's road trip adventure. The fake ID storyline became a template for later episodes exploring teenage independence and parental trust.

episodes.title: 22 Short Films About Springfield
episodes.description: Bart and Milhouse wonder if anything interesting happens to the citizens of Springfield, which leads to a series of interconnected vignettes.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.notes: This anthology-style episode pioneered a storytelling format that would influence later animated shows, with its interconnected vignettes structure directly inspiring episodes of Family Guy and American Dad.

episodes.title: Raging Abe Simpson and His Grumbling Grandson in 'The Curse of the Flying Hellfish'
episodes.description: When a fellow veteran dies, Grandpa Simpson and Mr. Burns fight for buried WWII treasure as the only surviving members of their battalion.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch as normal, but then the couch and family slide backward through the wall and end up in the neighbor's living room.
episodes.parody_of: The Treasure of the Sierra Madre and war films
episodes.notes: This episode reveals Mr. Burns' first name is Charles Montgomery Burns and establishes his surprising World War II military service, contradicting his previous characterization as a frail, ancient figure who avoided combat.

episodes.title: Much Apu About Nothing
episodes.description: When Mayor Quimby uses immigrants as a political scapegoat, the Simpsons discover that Apu is an illegal immigrant.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced Apu's full name — Apu Nahasapeemapetilon — and his backstory as an Indian immigrant who came to America to study computer science at Caltech.

episodes.title: Homerpalooza
episodes.description: To show his kids how hip he is, Homer takes Bart and Lisa to a music festival, where he earns himself a place in the sideshow by catching a cannonball with his belly.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch normally, but then the couch and family are revealed to be a painting that gets splattered with paint.
episodes.parody_of: Lollapalooza music festival and 1990s alternative rock culture
episodes.notes: Features guest voices from Smashing Pumpkins, Sonic Youth, and Cypress Hill performing as themselves, making it one of the most music-heavy episodes in the series' history.

episodes.title: Summer of 4 Ft. 2
episodes.description: On a family vacation, Lisa tries on a new identity to fit in with the other kids at the beach. Jealous, Bart reveals her true nerdiness to the group.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.notes: This episode marked one of the few times Lisa successfully reinvents herself socially, creating a rare character arc where she temporarily abandons her intellectual identity. The beach kids were voiced by actual teenagers, giving their dialogue an unusually authentic feel compared to typical adult voice actors playing children.

episodes.title: Treehouse of Horror VII
episodes.description: A Halloween trilogy. This time, Bart meets his "pure evil" Siamese twin; Lisa's science project attacks Bart; aliens Kang and Kodos invade as Clinton and Dole's replicas just before the 1996 Presidential election.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and sinks into quicksand as skeletons.
episodes.parody_of: The Thing with Two Heads, Basket Case, Invasion of the Body Snatchers
episodes.notes: The "Citizen Kang" segment features the first major political satire in Treehouse of Horror, with aliens Kang and Kodos impersonating Bill Clinton and Bob Dole during the actual 1996 presidential election cycle.

episodes.title: You Only Move Twice
episodes.description: In an idyllic planned community, Homer works for a new boss who has trouble with the government. Meanwhile, the rest of the family has difficulty dealing with the bucolic burg.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which is actually a wooden horse that crashes through the wall.
episodes.notes: Albert Brooks improvised most of his dialogue as Hank Scorpio, creating one of the series' most beloved one-off villains with his casual, friendly demeanor contrasting his Bond villain status.

episodes.title: The Homer They Fall
episodes.description: When Moe sees that Homer can absorb massive blows to the head, he turns him into a prizefighter and sets up a match with heavyweight champion Drederick Tatum.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which tips backward and launches them through the wall and into the night sky.
episodes.notes: Heavyweight champion Drederick Tatum was voiced by Hank Azaria doing an impression of Mike Tyson, complete with the distinctive lisp and intimidating demeanor that made him one of the show's most memorable one-off antagonists.

episodes.title: Burns, Baby Burns
episodes.description: Mr. Burns' boorish long-lost son Larry gets the brush-off from Burns, but Homer hatches a plan to reconcile them.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions, then shot out through the back.
episodes.notes: Rodney Dangerfield voiced Larry Burns in one of his few animated roles, bringing his signature "no respect" persona to Mr. Burns' estranged son. The episode's fake kidnapping plot directly parodies The Fugitive, complete with Homer and Larry handcuffed together fleeing through storm drains.

episodes.title: Bart After Dark
episodes.description: After trespassing and damaging property at a mysterious old house, Bart is required to work there as a punishment... but it turns out to be a burlesque house.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which folds into a bed with them on it.
episodes.notes: This episode features one of The Simpsons' most elaborate musical sequences, with the full-scale production number "We Put the Spring in Springfield" becoming a standout moment that showcased the series' Broadway-caliber musical ambitions.

episodes.title: A Milhouse Divided
episodes.description: Kirk and Luann Van Houten are getting a divorce. Homer is confident that it will never happen to him, but Kirk tells him how quickly things can change.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced Kirk Van Houten's post-divorce career as a single guy working at a cracker factory, spawning the memorable line "Can I borrow a feeling?" from his pathetic demo tape.

episodes.title: Lisa's Date with Density
episodes.description: Lisa develops a crush on Nelson and tries to change him into a better person. Meanwhile, Homer finds an autodialer and starts a telemarketing scam.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced Nelson's signature romantic gesture of giving Lisa a card reading "I Choo-Choo-Choose You" with a train on it, which became one of the most referenced Valentine's Day memes from the series.

episodes.title: Hurricane Neddy
episodes.description: A hurricane destroys the Flanders' home and they have to move into the church basement. The people of Springfield gather to rebuild their house, but when Ned sees the poor workmanship, he has a complete breakdown.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions, then shot out through the ceiling.
episodes.notes: This episode features Ned Flanders' only complete psychological breakdown in the series, revealing his repressed anger through a legendary rant that introduced "diddly" variations into Simpsons vernacular. The climactic scene where Ned calls Homer a "big fat pita" became one of the show's most quotable meltdown moments.

episodes.title: El Viaje Misterioso de Nuestro Jomer (The Mysterious Voyage of Homer)
episodes.description: After eating a dish laced with potent peppers during the chili cookoff, Homer has hallucinations where a mystical coyote urges him to find his soulmate.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions, then shot out through the back.
episodes.parody_of: Don Juan DeMarco and surreal vision quest narratives
episodes.notes: Johnny Cash voiced Homer's spirit guide coyote in one of the most surreal and acclaimed episodes of the series, marking the country legend's only appearance on The Simpsons.

episodes.title: The Springfield Files
episodes.description: Homer witnesses something out of this world in Springfield woods, but no one believes him -- not even FBI agents Fox Mulder and Dana Scully, who come to investigate the incident.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets beamed up by a tractor beam from a UFO above the house.
episodes.notes: David Duchovny and Gillian Anderson appeared as themselves playing Mulder and Scully, marking one of the most high-profile crossover guest appearances in animated television history at the time.

episodes.title: The Twisted World of Marge Simpson
episodes.description: Marge's new pretzel franchise is failing until Homer strikes a deal with Fat Tony, Springfield's local mobster.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch normally, but then the couch and family are revealed to be a painting that gets hung on the wall.
episodes.notes: This episode features one of Joe Mantegna's most memorable performances as Fat Tony, delivering the iconic line "I don't get mad, I get stabby" that became a fan-favorite Simpsons quote. The Goodfellas parody ending, with its freeze-frame narration style, is considered one of the show's most faithful and effective film homages.

episodes.title: Mountain of Madness
episodes.description: To promote teamwork amongst the power plant's employees, Mr. Burns makes them go on a corporate retreat up in the mountains.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which turns into a monster that swallows them, then burps up their bones.
episodes.notes: This episode features one of the most elaborate parodies of The Shining in TV history, with Homer and Mr. Burns recreating the film's descent into madness while trapped in a cabin during an avalanche.

episodes.title: Simpsoncalifragilisticexpiala (Annoyed Grunt) cious
episodes.description: Marge is stressed by the demands of motherhood and the Simpsons get a magical British nanny, who floats down from the sky holding an umbrella.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the TV, becoming part of various TV shows.
episodes.notes: This episode marked one of the rare occasions where The Simpsons produced a full musical parody, featuring eight original songs that closely mirrored the structure and style of Disney's Mary Poppins soundtrack.

episodes.title: The Itchy & Scratchy & Poochie Show
episodes.description: In an attempt to boost ratings, the Itchy and Scratchy Show adds a new "in-your-face" character voiced by Homer.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch which folds into a bed, and they all fall asleep.
episodes.parody_of: TV industry focus groups and character creation
episodes.notes: This meta-commentary episode introduced Poochie's catchphrase "I have to go now, my planet needs me" and his abrupt off-screen death, which became shorthand for poorly executed character exits in TV shows.

episodes.title: Homer's Phobia
episodes.description: The Simpsons befriend a shop owner, but when Homer discovers the man is gay, he worries about his influence on Bart.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch normally, but then the couch and family are revealed to be a painting that gets hung in a museum.
episodes.notes: This groundbreaking episode won a GLAAD Media Award and featured John Waters in his first voice acting role, bringing his distinctive Baltimore accent to the character of John.

episodes.title: Brother from Another Series
episodes.description: When Sideshow Bob is released from prison, his brother Cecil puts him in charge of building Springfield's new hydroelectric dam.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch normally, but then the couch and family are revealed to be a painting that gets hung on the wall.
episodes.notes: Kelsey Grammer's real-life brother David Hyde Pierce voices Cecil, Sideshow Bob's brother, mirroring their roles as the Crane brothers on Frasier.

episodes.title: My Sister, My Sitter
episodes.description: Lisa has a stellar reputation as a babysitter, but when Homer and Marge leave her in charge of Bart and Maggie, everything starts going wrong.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which is actually a tongue that pulls them into a monster's mouth.
episodes.notes: Features the debut of Dr. Hibbert's notorious diaper-changing technique and Lisa's first major babysitting disaster, establishing her perfectionist streak that would become a recurring character trait in later seasons.

episodes.title: Homer vs. the Eighteenth Amendment
episodes.description: When Prohibition is revived in Springfield, Homer and Bart supply the town with alcohol.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch which is actually a slot machine that spins them around and pays out coins.
episodes.notes: Introduces Rex Banner, the federal agent parody of Eliot Ness from The Untouchables, voiced by Joe Mantegna in one of his most memorable guest appearances. The episode's Prohibition-era pastiche became a template for the show's later period parodies.

episodes.title: Grade School Confidential
episodes.description: Bart discovers Principal Skinner and Mrs. Krabappel kissing, and it's not long before the whole town knows of their secret affair.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.notes: This episode established the long-running romantic relationship between Principal Skinner and Mrs. Krabappel, which would become a recurring storyline throughout the series for over a decade.

episodes.title: The Canine Mutiny
episodes.description: When Bart can't pay the credit card bill for his expensive new purebred dog, the repo men unwittingly take Santa's Little Helper.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one until only their eyes are visible.
episodes.notes: This episode marks the only time in the series where Santa's Little Helper is temporarily replaced by another dog, making it a rare instance where the Simpson family pet roster changes.

episodes.title: The Old Man and the Lisa
episodes.description: Financially ruined, Mr. Burns turns to Lisa for help in building a recycling business. Lisa believes he's changed, but his new business venture takes a shocking new direction.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which folds into a bed with the family tucked in under blankets.
episodes.parody_of: The Old Man and the Sea
episodes.notes: This episode features one of the series' most shocking plot twists when Burns' seemingly wholesome recycling operation is revealed to be a brutal slurry machine that kills sea life, subverting audience expectations in a way that became a template for later episodes.

episodes.title: In Marge We Trust
episodes.description: Disturbed that Reverend Lovejoy has lost interest in counseling parishioners, Marge volunteers as the "Listen Lady." Meanwhile, Homer sees his likeness on a box of Japanese detergent.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch normally, but then the couch and family are revealed to be a dollhouse being played with by a giant Maggie.
episodes.notes: Introduced the recurring character of Mr. Sparkle, Homer's Japanese doppelganger from a detergent box, which became one of the show's most beloved one-off gags despite minimal screen time.

episodes.title: Homer's Enemy
episodes.description: A hardworking new employee at the power plant is consumed with resentment at Homer's undeserved success.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions, then shot out through the back.
episodes.notes: Features the only appearance of Frank Grimes, whose tragic story became a fan touchstone for the show's shift in tone.

episodes.title: The Simpsons Spin-Off Showcase
episodes.description: Troy McClure presents three Simpsons spinoff spoofs: In "Chief Wiggum, P.I.," Chief Wiggum becomes a New Orleans detective. Next, Grampa Simpson's soul is trapped inside Moe's Love tester machine in "The Love-Matic Grampa." Finally, the Simpson family hosts a '70s-style variety show.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the TV screen.
episodes.parody_of: TV variety shows and spin-off culture
episodes.notes: This clip show parody marked one of the few times The Simpsons directly satirized the TV industry's spin-off culture, arriving at the peak of successful sitcom spin-offs like Frasier and Boy Meets World

episodes.title: The Secret War of Lisa Simpson
episodes.description: Lisa joins the all-male military school where Bart is sent for discipline. Then comes the hazing.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets flipped upside down, with their hair hanging down.
episodes.parody_of: Military school films and hazing rituals
episodes.notes: This marked one of the earliest episodes to focus primarily on Lisa's character development and feminism, helping establish her as the show's moral center. The military school setting allowed the writers to explore themes of gender equality and institutional sexism that would become recurring elements in later Lisa-centric episodes.
